markdown:
1. Since achieving **equity** is a desirable **outcome **for the **economy**, measuring the **income and wealth inequalities** present in it is essential.** **
2. There are two main **tools** economists use to measure and display **income and wealth inequalities**:
  1. **The Lorenz curve.**
  2. **The Gini coefficient.**

<callout type="note">Note that in theory the income shares can be divided into any percentage, depending on the needs.</callout>

## The Lorenz curve


<definition term="Lorenz curve">A **graphical** **representation** of the **income** **inequality** within an economy.</definition>

An economy's population can be divided into **income quintiles**:

1. **Income quintiles **divide an economy's population into groups of **20%** of the population, ordered from **poorest** (least income-earners) to **richest** (highest income-earners).
2. The economy's population is therefore divided into **five quintiles **(five 20%), ranging from the poorest 20% to the richest 20%.
3. This way, it can be seen how different is the income distributed within a country (_Table 1_).

| Quintile | Poorest 20% | Second 20% | Third 20% | Fourth 20% | Richest 20% |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| % of Total Income | 5% | 10% | 15% | 20% | 50% |
| Income Decimal Index  | 0.05 | 0.1 | 0.15 | 0.2 | 0.5 |

1. _Table 1_ above shows how:
  1. The **poorest** **20%** only earns **5%** of the **total income**.
  2. The **second** **20%** earns **10%** of the **total income**.
  3. The **third** **20%** earns **15%** of the **total income**.
  4. The **fourth 20% **earns **20% **of the **total income**.
  5. The **richest** **20%** earns **50%** of the **total income **(half of the economy's income is earned by the top 20% the population).
2. The **Lorenz curve** (_Figure 1_) can be used to represent this data:

![Figure 1: Lorenz Curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/a2a8fb835b139e10d521f7c1fa6d47e1d8ebe1ab-2368x1794.png)

A **Lorenz curve** represents the **income inequality of an economy** in the following way:

1. **Axis** representation of **population** and **income**:
  1. The **y axis** represents the **cumulative percentage of total income earned in the economy (0% to 100%)**.** **
  2. The **x axis **represents the **cumulative percentage of population in the economy** **(0% to 100%)**.** **
2. The **cumulative income percentage** is **plotted** **against** the **income quintiles**, and joined by a line of best fit, making the **Lorenz curve**.
3. A **diagonal line (line of perfect equality) **is drawn for reference, connects the left bottom and top right corners. This would be the **Lorenz curve **of an economy with **perfect income equality**.

<callout type="note">If there was **perfect equality, each income quintile would receive the 20% of the total income in the country.**</callout>

1. The **Lorenz curve** can be used to **compare** **income** **distributions** **over** **time** or **between** **countries**.
2. This helps **policymakers** assess the **effectiveness** of **interventions** aimed at reducing **inequality**.

<callout type="tip">Remember that **the closer the Lorenz Curve is to the line of perfect equality, the higher the income equality of the country **(or lower inequality)**.**</callout>

## The Gini Coefficient


<definition term="Gini coefficient">A **numerical** **representation** of the information given by the **Lorenz** **Curve**, measuring **income** or **wealth** **inequality** on an **index** from **0 **(**perfect** **equality**) to **1** (**perfect inequality**).</definition>

1. The **Gini coefficient essentially** represents the income **inequality** **information** given by the **Lorenz curve**, in a numerical manner.
2. The **Gini coefficient** is an index **between 0 to 1**, where:
  1. **Values closer to 0 signify more equality in income/wealth distribution**
  2. **Values closer to 1 signify greater income/wealth inequality**.

![Figure 2: calculation of the Gini coefficient from the Lorenz Curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ddfb59b4995c3c5be3472986adedb6ab901c4837-2368x1794.png)

** **As it can be seen in _Figure 2_ above:

1. The **Lorenz curve diagram** is **divided** into two areas: **A** **and** **B**.
2. The **formula** for the **Gini coefficient** is then:

$$\text{Gini Coefficient} = \frac{A}{A + B}$$

<callout type="note">1. Note that all of these **methods** (quintiles, Lorenz curve, Gini coefficient), can** **also be used to calculate** wealth inequality**.
2. It is also interesting to note that **wealth inequality is usually higher than income inequality**. This is because:
    1. Higher income people are able to save more of their income and accumulate wealth.
    2. The slow growth in wages hinders the accumulation of wealth by the poor.</callout>

<callout type="tok">Is it even possible to represent inequalities mathematically?</callout>
